it's literally a config file and some instructions. people are buying it.
i have 33 autonomous agents, 310 automation scripts, and a full orchestration system running 24/7.
the productization of developer workflows is a real market. most devs are terrible at setting up their tools. sell the setup, not the tool.
it tells you if your hardware can run a specific AI model.
the genius: affiliate links to GPU retailers when the answer is "no."
"can you do X?" sites are underrated. simple boolean answer + affiliate upsell when the answer is negative. works for hardware, software, anything with a purchase decision.
when two independent teams ship the same idea simultaneously, the market is screaming for it.
agent orchestration isn't a feature anymore. it's a category.
that's roughly 3,000 pages of text in a single prompt.
most people will use this to summarize documents.
the real play: feed it your entire codebase + all your docs + all your data and let it find patterns humans can't see.
context window size isn't about reading more. it's about connecting dots across larger datasets.
the comments are losing their minds
but here's the thing. $40k/mo is $480k/year. that's generational comfort money. no investors. no board. no growth-at-all-costs pressure.
the real flex isn't the number. it's the decision to stop chasing.

4 were a complete waste of time.
3 actually worked.
most people would keep all 7 running "just in case." that's how you burn out at $500 MRR.
the move is always: test fast, kill losers, double winners.
not magic. not virality. not luck.
they just went where the users already were and solved a problem people were actively complaining about.
the best distribution channel is already-frustrated people.
this post is getting traction because everyone knows it's true but nobody wants to hear it.

you vibe-coded an app in 4 hours. cool. what happens when:
- the AI model changes
- your prompt stops working
- you need to debug actual logic
the app is the easy part. maintaining it is the real game.

1/5
there's a growing movement of SaaS founders who hit $30-50k MRR and deliberately stop growing.
not because they can't scale. because they don't want to.
here's why this is the most underrated strategy in tech:

2/5
at $40k MRR you're making $480k/year. probably 70-80% margins if you're solo.
that's $336-384k take-home. no employees. no office. no investors.
most VCs would call this a "lifestyle business" like it's an insult.

3/5
the math on scaling past $40k MRR:
- hire 2-3 people: -$200k/year
- office/tools: -$50k/year
- your stress: 3x
- your time: goes from 20hrs/week to 60
net result: you make LESS money working MORE hours

4/5
the founders doing this aren't lazy. they're running the numbers.
$384k/year working 20 hours/week = $370/hour effective rate

scale to $100k MRR with a team of 5?
$1.2M revenue - $600k costs = $600k profit
but now you're working 60hrs/week
= $192/hour
you literally made less per hour by "succeeding"

5/5
the real alpha: build something that hits $30-50k MRR. stop. enjoy your life.
use the extra time to build another $30k MRR product.
